answers.answer: Under Article 28(1) of the General Partnership Law 2004, each Partner is liable jointly and severally with the other Partners for all debts and obligations of the General Partnership incurred while he is a Partner, unless otherwise agreed by all the other Partners.
The Board of Directors of the DIFCA is responsible for appointing and dismissing the Registrar.
The effective date for due diligence requirements under the Common Reporting Standard Law 2018 is 31 December, 2016 for Pre-existing Accounts and 1 January, 2017 for New Accounts.
The Law was made by the Ruler.
The Employer is required to keep records of the Employee's Remuneration (gross and net, where applicable), and the applicable Pay Period.
The No Costs Application is dismissed, and there shall be no order as to costs for the No Costs Application.

The retention periods for records according to Article 12(4) of the Common Reporting Standard Law and Article 18(2)(b) of the General Partnership Law are as follows: 

- Common Reporting Standard Law: null
- General Partnership Law: at least six (6) years from the date upon which they were created.
The application in case CFI 067/2025 was dismissed, and the Applicant was ordered to pay the Respondent's costs of the Application in the total sum of USD 155,879.50.
The court's final ruling in case CFI 010/2024 is that the Claimant's Application for a Default Costs Certificate is dismissed.
The Court of Appeal ruled that the Appeal must be allowed to the extent ordered, and the Judge made no order as to costs.

The jury decided to allow the Appellant Judgment Creditor’s appeal, setting aside the previous order and restoring the order sought by the Appellant in the enforcement proceedings. The Respondents were ordered to pay the Appellant’s costs of the Appeal assessed at USD 60,541.25.
The court decided that the Injunctions shall remain in place until further order.
The application for permission to appeal was refused.
The Defendants' application for immediate judgment was dismissed, except for certain claims for breach of fiduciary duty which were struck out.
The Council administers the Foundations Law.

The purpose of Article 9(1) of the Foundations Law is to allow a Foreign Foundation that wishes to conduct operations within the DIFC to apply for a Licence to be recognized as a Recognised Foreign Foundation in accordance with the law.
